heroTitle: Wake up at the right moment
heroText1: This tool helps you calculate when to go to bed or wake up based on sleep cycles of about 90 minutes.
heroText2: It is not a medical tool, but a simple practical calculator for planning your sleep schedule.
wakeTitle: Calculate the best time to go to bed
wakeText: Enter when you need to wake up, and the site will show the best bedtime options.
wakeTimeLabel: I need to wake up at
sleepText: Enter when you plan to go to bed, and the site will suggest the best wake-up times.
durationText: Check how much sleep you get, how many cycles it includes, and whether it is convenient.
napText: A short nap can restore energy, but it is better not to wake up in the middle of deep sleep.
routineText: The site can build a simple evening plan: when to reduce screens, slow down, and go to bed.
allTipsText: Tips are shown randomly on the main page, but you can view the full list here.
footerText: Sleep Cycle Calculator is a simple sleep planning tool. It does not replace medical advice.
durationAdviceBad: Try shifting bedtime or wake-up time a little to get closer to a full cycle.
durationAdviceGood: This time is close to the end of a full sleep cycle.
napGoodNote: This can help you restore energy quickly.
napFullNote: 90 minutes is a full nap sleep cycle option.
napLongNote: A long nap may make it harder to fall asleep at night.

normal: Shower, reading, or a calm routine
deep: Avoid coffee, heavy food, and intense activity
Calm routine: shower, book, quiet time
Prepare the room: dark, cool, quiet

tips: Go to bed and wake up at about the same time every day, including weekends.
Start a calm evening routine 45–90 minutes before sleep.
Put away your phone, laptop, and TV at least 30–60 minutes before bed.
Keep your bedroom dark, quiet, and cool.
Avoid heavy meals right before bed; try finishing dinner 2–3 hours earlier.
Avoid caffeine in the afternoon or evening, especially if you are sensitive to it.
Regular daytime physical activity can help you fall asleep more easily.
Do not use your bed as a workplace — help your brain associate it with sleep and rest.
If you cannot fall asleep after about 20 minutes, get up and do something quiet in dim light.
Alcohol may help you fall asleep, but it often worsens sleep quality during the night.
Limit large amounts of liquid before bedtime to reduce night-time bathroom trips.
Get daylight in the morning or during the day to support your body clock.
Try reading, calm music, breathing exercises, or meditation before sleep.
Use a comfortable mattress, pillow, and bedding that do not overheat your body.
If sleep problems last a long time or strongly affect your life, it is better to speak with a doctor.
